perturbation.original_text: 5.2.1 Subject to sub-Clause 5.2.5 below and in the event the Representatives (i) fail to create such committee or (ii) do not agree on an amicable settlement within [*****] from the date the committee referred to in this sub-Clause 5.2 has been created or such longer period as may be agreed upon in writing by the Representatives (the "Amicable Settlement Period"), the Dispute shall be exclusively and finally settled under the Rules and Conciliation of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce (the "ICC") by an arbitral tribunal composed of three (3) arbitrators; each Party shall then appoint one (1) arbitrator within [*****]from the last day of the Amicable Settlement Period and the third arbitrator, who will act as President, will be appointed by the other two (2) arbitrators. In case the two (2) arbitrators appointed by the Parties do not agree on this choice with [*****] from the date the last arbitrator is appointed, the third arbitrator will be appointed by the ICC Court.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2.1 Subject to sub-Clause 5.2.5 below and in the event the Representatives (i) fail to create such committee or (ii) do not agree on an amicable settlement within [*****] from the date the committee referred to in this sub-Clause 5.2 has been created or such longer period as may be agreed upon in writing by the Representatives (the "Amicable Settlement Period"), the Dispute shall be exclusively and finally settled under the Rules and Conciliation of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce (the "ICC") by an arbitral tribunal composed of three (3) arbitrators; each Party shall then appoint one (1) arbitrator within 10 days from the last day of the Amicable Settlement Period and the third arbitrator, who will act as President, will be appointed by the other two (2) arbitrators. In case the two (2) arbitrators appointed by the Parties do not agree on this choice with 5 days from the date the last arbitrator is appointed, the third arbitrator will be appointed by the ICC Court.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for a period of time (redacted) for the appointment of arbitrators. This change imposes an unrealistically short deadline of 10 days for each party to appoint an arbitrator and 5 days for the two arbitrators to agree on the third arbitrator, which is unlikely to be compliant with NY law on arbitration agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CVP) § 7504
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 7504 provides for court intervention to designate an arbitrator if the agreed method fails or if there is a lapse in time. By setting an unreasonably short timeframe, the parties are practically ensuring that the method for selecting an arbitrator will fail, necessitating court intervention, which could be argued as a violation of the intent to arbitrate privately and efficiently. While not a direct violation, it undermines the process contemplated by the statute for selecting arbitrators in a timely manner and creates a situation where court intervention is likely.

Court appointment of arbitrator. If the arbitration agreement does not provide for a method of appointment of an arbitrator, or if the agreed method fails or for any reason is not followed, or if an arbitrator fails to act and his successor has not been appointed, the court, on application of a party, shall appoint an arbitrator.

perturbation.justification: The changed text sets unrealistically short deadlines for arbitrator appointments (10 days and 5 days), potentially forcing court intervention. This contradicts the intent of private and efficient arbitration, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both reference NY CPLR § 7504, which states that if the agreed method for arbitrator appointment fails, the court will appoint one. The Perturbation Explanation supports that the new deadlines undermine the agreed upon method and are thus inconsistent. This qualifies as an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.6 This Clause 4 shall survive termination or expiry of this Amendment for a period of five (5) years following such End Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.6 This Clause 4 shall survive termination or expiry of this Amendment for a period of one (1) year following such End Date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year might be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, especially for agreements involving complex technical data and industrial processes. This introduces a contradiction.
perturbation.law_citation: Ashland Management Inc. v. Janien, 82 N.Y.2d 395 (N.Y. 1993)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no specifically defined statutory period for trade secret protection in New York, courts consider the "head start" a competitor gains through misappropriation and the time it would take to independently develop the information. A one-year period may be deemed insufficient, undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets, which is implicitly acknowledged by the original five-year term.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Ashland Mgt. v. Janien :: 1993 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Ashland Mgt. v. Janien Annotate this Case 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993) 624 N.E.2d 1007 604 N.Y.S.2d 912 Ashland Management Incorporated, Appellant, v. C. Christopher Janien, Respondent. (And a Third-Party Action.) Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued October 14, 1993. Decided November 22, 1993. Paul, Hastings, Janofsky & Walker, New York City (Lawrence I. Weinstein, Lisa M. Buckley and Roger M. Milgrim of counsel), for appellant. Davis & Gilbert, New York City (Michael C. Lasky, Bruce M. Ginsberg and Howard Weingrad of counsel), for respondent. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges TITONE, HANCOCK, JR., BELLACOSA, SMITH and LEVINE concur. *399SIMONS, J. This litigation involves a dispute between an investment management firm and a former employee. The employee developed a mathematical model for determining investment strategy and sought unsuccessfully to sell it to the firm. A dispute arose during the negotiations and plaintiff, Ashland Management Inc., instituted this action seeking to foreclose defendant from using his model. It alleged that the model is based upon trade secrets defendant misappropriated from the firm. Defendant claims he and plaintiff had agreed upon a contract for the use of the model and that plaintiff breached the contract. Defendant, therefore, counterclaimed seeking damages. After a nonjury trial, Supreme Court found that the parties had entered into a contract and that plaintiff had breached it. Accordingly, the court awarded defendant damages of $625,000 for lost profits. It held further that the information available to defendant did not constitute a trade secret of Ashland and therefore defendant was not guilty of misappropriation. The Appellate Division, after a minor modification, affirmed the judgment. The principal legal questions presented concern the availability of damages for lost profits under the rule stated in our two decisions in Ken
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the survival period of Clause 4 from five years to one year, which the perturbation explanation suggests may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not available, Scraped Law Snippet 1, Ashland Mgt. v. Janien, discusses trade secret misappropriation in New York. The Law Explanation also notes that courts consider the 'head start' a competitor gains. Reducing the protection period could undermine the original intent of protecting trade secrets, potentially contradicting legal expectations for adequate protection.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2.3 The arbitration shall be kept confidential and the existence of the proceeding and any element of it shall not be disclosed to any third party. Any information relating to and/or documents generated for the purpose of or produced in the arbitration, including any awards, shall remain confidential
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2.3 The arbitration shall be kept confidential and the existence of the proceeding and any element of it shall not be disclosed to any third party. Any information relating to and/or documents generated for the purpose of or produced in the arbitration, including any awards, shall remain confidential unless either party is compelled to disclose the same by legal process, in which case, only such information as is strictly required to be disclosed need be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: While it seems reasonable to allow for disclosure if 'compelled by legal process,' removing the clause that the parties only disclose what is strictly required could lead to a breach of confidentiality that is more than required by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (New York)
perturbation.law_citation: Dalton v. Educational Testing Serv., 87 N.Y.2d 384 (1995)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, all contracts contain an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This means neither party can act in a way that deprives the other of the benefits of the agreement. The removal of the condition to disclose only what is strictly required creates a loop hole that allows either party to disclose much more information than what the court requires, and it is not considered good faith or fair dealing. The implied covenant is a legal requirement for any contract in New York.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Dalton v. Educ. Testing Serv. :: 1995 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Dalton v. Educ. Testing Serv. Annotate this Case 87 N.Y.2d 384 (1995) 663 N.E.2d 289 639 N.Y.S.2d 977 Peter Dalton, as Parent and Natural Guardian of Brian M. Dalton, an Infant, Respondent, v. Educational Testing Service, Appellant. Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued October 19, 1995 Decided December 7, 1995. White & Case, New York City (Philip H. Schaeffer, J. Stepan Wood and William R. Spiegelberger of counsel), and Wilmer, Cutler & Pickering (Bruce Berman and Michael F. Bennet, of the District of Columbia Bar, admitted pro hac vice, of counsel), for appellant. Nicolosi & Sciacca, Bayside (Vincent F. Nicolosi and Laurie S. Hershey of counsel), for respondent. Judges TITONE, BELLACOSA, SMITH and CIPARICK concur with Chief Judge KAYE; Judge LEVINE dissents and votes to reverse in a separate opinion in which Judge SIMONS concurs. *386Chief Judge KAYE. The primary question before us is whether defendant, Educational Testing Service (ETS), a standardized testing firm, complied with procedures specified in its contract with high school senior Brian Dalton in refusing to release Dalton's Scholastic Aptitude Test (SAT) score. Because the factual findings underlying the trial court's determination that ETS failed to act in good faith in following those procedures were affirmed by the Appellate Division, have support in the record and are consequently beyond the scope of our review, we conclude as did the trial court and Appellate Division that ETS breached its contract with Dalton. Though we agree, moreover, with the *387 courts below that specific performance is the appropriate remedy, we nevertheless conclude that the promised performance was good-faith compliance with the stated procedures, not release of the questioned scores as ordered by those courts. I In May 1991, Brian Dalton took the SAT, which was administered by ETS, at Holy Cross High School in Que
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by removing the clause that only strictly required information be disclosed, contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation states that removing this condition 'creates a loop hole that allows either party to disclose much more information than what the court requires, and it is not considered good faith or fair dealing'. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where a contract sets a requirement that violates state law (implied covenant), making the terms unenforceable.
